revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with '{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Jap...'
revision.text:
{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tianjin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tianjin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tianjin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tianjin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>
== See also == *[[Japanese concession in Tianjin]] *[[Yamato Park]]
== References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in China]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in Tianjin]] [[Category:1920 establishments in Tianjin]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in China]] [[Category:Foreign concessions in Tianjin]] [[Category:Former British colonies and protectorates in Asia]] [[Category:20th century in Tianjin]] [[Category:China–Japan relations]]

{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tianjin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tianjin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tianjin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tianjin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>
== See also == *[[Japanese concession in Tianjin]] *[[Yamato Park]]
== References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in China]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in Tianjin]] [[Category:1920 establishments in Tianjin]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in China]] [[Category:Foreign concessions in Tianjin]] [[Category:Former British colonies and protectorates in Asia]] [[Category:20th century in Tianjin]] [[Category:China–Japan relations]]

{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tianjin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tianjin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tianjin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tianjin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>
== See also == *[[Japanese concession in Tianjin]] *[[Yamato Park]]
== References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in China]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in Tianjin]] [[Category:1920 establishments in Tianjin]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in China]] [[Category:Foreign concessions in Tianjin]] [[Category:Former British colonies and protectorates in Asia]] [[Category:20th century in Tianjin]] [[Category:China–Japan relations]] [[Category:Qq]]{{wikidata link|Q133857952}}[[ja:天津神社 (中国)]][[zh:天津神社]][[Category:qq]]{{commons category]]

{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tianjin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tianjin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tianjin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tianjin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>

== References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in China]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in Tianjin]] [[Category:1920 establishments in Tianjin]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in China]] [[Category:Foreign concessions in Tianjin]] [[Category:Former British colonies and protectorates in Asia]] [[Category:20th century in Tianjin]] [[Category:China–Japan relations]] [[Category:Qq]]{{wikidata link|Q133857952}}[[ja:天津神社 (中国)]][[zh:天津神社]][[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q133857952}}[[ja:天津神社 (中国)]][[zh:天津神社]][[Category:qq]]{{commons category|Tientsin Shrine}}[[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox building | name = Tianjin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tianjin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tianjin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tianjin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tianjin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tianjin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tianjin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>

== References == {{reflist}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in China]] [[Category:Demolished buildings and structures in Tianjin]] [[Category:1920 establishments in Tianjin]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in China]] [[Category:Foreign concessions in Tianjin]] [[Category:Former British colonies and protectorates in Asia]] [[Category:20th century in Tianjin]] [[Category:China–Japan relations]] [[Category:Qq]]{{wikidata link|Q133857952}}[[ja:天津神社 (中国)]][[zh:天津神社]][[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q133857952}}[[ja:天津神社 (中国)]][[zh:天津神社]][[Category:qq]]{{commons category|Tientsin Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Qq]][[en:Tianjin Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox building | name = Tientsin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tientsin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tientsin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tientsin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tientsin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tientsin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tientsin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>

{{Infobox building | name = Tientsin Shrine | native_name = 天津神社 | image = File:Solemn Tientsin Shrine.jpg | location = Yamato Park, Japanese Concession, Tianjin, China | completion_date = 1920 | demolition_date = 1945 | architectural_style = ''Shinmei-zukuri'' }} Tientsin Shrine (天津神社), also known as the Tianjin Japanese Shrine, was a Shinto place of worship located in the modern-era [[Japanese concession in Tianjin|Tianjin Japanese Concession]], inside [[Yamato Park]]. The shrine was dedicated to Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Emperor Meiji, and its buildings adopted the traditional Japanese Shinmei-zukuri architectural style.<ref>{{cite book |editor=Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |title=1928 Administrative Report of the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin |location=Tianjin |year=1928 |language=ja}}</ref> It stood at the northwest corner of today's intersection of Anshan Road and Shandong Road. In 1945, following Japan's defeat in the war, Tientsin Shrine—regarded as a symbol of the Japanese invasion of China—was demolished.
== History == [[File:Twenty Views of Tianjin - Tientsin Shrine.jpg|thumb|Tientsin Shrine on a postcard]] In 1915, the Japanese Residents' Association in Tianjin resolved to establish Tientsin Shrine in Yamato Park of the Japanese Concession to celebrate the enthronement ceremonies of Emperor Taishō. Flooding later caused delays in construction, and the shrine was not completed until 1920.<ref name="大陸神社大観">{{cite book |author1=Iwata Denshirō |title=Survey of Shrines in Mainland Asia |year=1941 |publisher=Continental Shinto Federation |location=Keijō (modern Seoul) |page=506}}</ref>
In 1945, after Japan's defeat, the shrine was dismantled as a symbol of Japanese aggression in China.
On April 27, 2014, according to an on-site investigation by Japanese researchers, no trace of the shrine remains; the original site has since been redeveloped into a PetroChina gas station.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://kanagawa-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/record/8912/files/10%20news%20letter33.pdf |title=Sites of Former Shrines in North China (Beijing, Tianjin, Jinan, Yantai, Qingdao) |author1=Inamiya Yasuto |publisher=Kanagawa University |access-date=2025-04-15 |language=ja }}</ref>
